title: Fitting a washing machine inlet and waste
subtitle: Connect the cold inlet and standpipe waste so a new washing machine fills and drains correctly
excerpt: How to connect a new washing machine to the cold supply with the supplied inlet hose, and to the waste with a standpipe trap. Covers checking the existing isolating valve, fitting the new hose, and the standpipe height required for the trap to break vacuum.

glossaryTerms.definition: A vertical pipe behind a washing machine into which the drain hose is hooked. The standpipe rises 600 mm above the trap to break vacuum and stop the machine siphoning out fill water.
A quarter-turn brass valve fitted on the cold supply pipe at the washing machine connection point. Allows the machine to be removed without isolating the whole house cold supply.

content.text: A new washing machine fits in 20 minutes if the cold supply and waste are already in place. The inlet uses the supplied hose; the waste uses the existing
standpipe
. The job needs no tools beyond an adjustable spanner for the hose fittings if the inlet is brass.
Step 1, check the existing connections
Look behind the machine space for a blue-handled
appliance isolating valve

on the cold supply pipe. Find the standpipe, which is a vertical 38 mm plastic pipe with an open top, behind or beside the machine space. If either is missing, that part of the work is a separate job that has to come first.
Step 2, close the isolating valve
Turn the lever on the valve a quarter-turn so the handle lies across the pipe. The cold supply is now off. If a hose is already connected to the valve, undo it by hand from the valve outlet.
Step 3, fit the inlet hose to the valve

The supplied inlet hose has a 90-degree fitting on one end and a straight fitting on the other. Hand-screw the straight end onto the isolating valve outlet. The fitting has a rubber washer inside; do not add PTFE tape. Hand-tight plus a quarter-turn with the adjustable spanner is enough.
Step 4, fit the inlet hose to the machine

Screw the 90-degree end onto the threaded inlet at the back of the machine. The fitting is plastic; hand-tighten only. The 90-degree bend lets the hose run down the wall to the floor before turning into the machine, which avoids kinks.
Step 5, hook the drain hose into the standpipe

Find the drain hose at the back of the machine; it has a moulded hook at the open end. Slide the supplied plastic hook clip over the standpipe. Hook the drain hose into the clip so the open end sits 50 to 75 mm down inside the standpipe. The top of the hook must be 600 mm above the trap behind the standpipe; this height breaks vacuum and stops the machine siphoning out.
Step 6, open the valve and check

Turn the isolating valve lever in line with the pipe. Check the connections at both ends of the inlet hose with a dry tissue. A damp mark means a weep; tighten the joint a sixth of a turn at a time until it stops. Plug the machine into the socket and run a hot-fill cycle on empty to flush the new hose and confirm the drain sits in the standpipe correctly.
Variations

A hot-and-cold-fill machine takes two inlet hoses. The red-handled hot valve fits the hot inlet at the back of the machine, the blue-handled cold valve fits the cold inlet. Most modern machines are cold-fill only; check the machine instructions. A dishwasher uses the same standpipe with its own hook clip; the inlet uses a separate isolating valve from the washing machine cold.
Care

Inlet hoses degrade over years of pressure and should be replaced every five years. A burst inlet hose floods the kitchen in minutes; spend a few pounds on a fresh hose well before the failure. Close the isolating valve when the machine is not in use for a long period.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Machine drains as it fills (water siphons out)
attrs.cause: Drain hose hook is below 600 mm above the trap, breaking the air gap.
attrs.fix: Raise the standpipe or fit a longer hose hook so the top of the bend sits 600 mm above the trap.

attrs.symptom: Slow leak at the inlet hose connection on the valve
attrs.cause: Rubber washer not seated, cross-threaded fitting, or the valve outlet has a worn O-ring.
attrs.fix: Close the valve. Undo the hose, check the washer is centred and flat, refit hand-tight plus a quarter-turn. If the leak continues, replace the valve.

attrs.symptom: Standpipe overflows during the drain cycle
attrs.cause: Standpipe trap is partially blocked, or the drain hose sits too deep in the pipe.
attrs.fix: Remove the trap from the wall, clean the U-bend, refit. Raise the drain hose so the open end sits no more than 75 mm inside the standpipe.
